context_pointers.vague_reference: maintain consistency with the sorting logic used in the request body serialization module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The output keys must be sorted in ascending alphabetical order, matching the pattern implemented in the standard serialization utility.

context_pointers.vague_reference: adhere to the protocol negotiation strategy defined in the HTTP/1.1 handshake helper
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The default protocol version must be set to 1.1 when not explicitly overridden, following the established pattern for legacy HTTP compatibility.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that — check the codebase or the existing test cases for the exact behavior.

feature10_pipeline_middleware.json.description: Compose middleware around an inner handler with a pipeline. Each middleware may inspect the request before the inner handler runs and the response afterwards; request processing runs outermost-first and response processing runs innermost-first. A middleware request phase may short-circuit by returning a response, in which case the inner handler and response phase are skipped. A response phase may replace the response. An error thrown by the inner handler is offered to a middleware error phase, which may produce a replacement response or rethrow; errors from the request or response phases bypass the error phase. A hijack signal always propagates untouched.

cases.expected_output:
request:A
request:B
handler
response:B
response:A
status=200
body=ok

feature11_chunked_encoding.json.description: Wrap a handler with middleware that applies chunked transfer coding to streamed responses. Chunked coding is added (and the transfer-encoding header set) only when the response has no known content length, has a normal (>=200, not 204, not 304) status, is not multipart/byteranges, and does not already use a non-identity transfer-encoding. Otherwise the response passes through unchanged.

feature1_header_model.json.description: Construct an HTTP message from a header map whose values may be a single string or a list of strings, then inspect both header views. The single-valued view maps each name to its values joined by commas; the multi-valued view preserves the list per name. Header names are case-insensitive. A message built with no body and no explicit headers carries a default content-length of 0.

cases.expected_output:
headers[a]=A
headers[b]=B1,B2
headers[content-length]=0
headersAll[a]=["A"]
headersAll[b]=["B1","B2"]
headersAll[content-length]=["0"]

feature2_content_length.json.description: Determine the content length of a message from its body and headers. A string or byte body yields the byte length of the encoded body and always takes precedence over any content-length header. A body whose length cannot be determined (a stream) yields no length unless a content-length header supplies one. A non-identity transfer-encoding suppresses the computed length; an identity transfer-encoding does not.

feature3_content_negotiation.json.description: Derive the MIME type and character encoding of a message from its content-type header, and observe how supplying an explicit body encoding rewrites that header. The MIME type is the bare media type without parameters. The encoding comes from the charset parameter and is absent when there is no charset or the charset is unknown. When an explicit encoding is given, the charset of the content-type is set accordingly, defaulting the media type to application/octet-stream when no content-type was present.

feature4_body_read.json.description: Read the body of a message. Reading as text decodes the bytes using, in order of precedence, an explicit encoding argument, the content-type charset, then UTF-8. Reading as raw bytes returns the body unchanged. A string body is encoded to bytes using the message encoding. The body may be read only once; a second read fails with a neutral state error.

cases.expected_output:
first=read
error=invalid_state
message=The 'read' method can only be called once on a shelf.Request/shelf.Response object.

feature5_message_change.json.description: Create a copy of a message applying targeted changes. A header or context entry set to null is removed; set to an empty list is removed; set to a string or list replaces the value. New entries are added. Passing an empty change set returns the same underlying header and context instances. The body can be replaced with a new value.

cases.expected_output:
headers[content-length]=0
headers[header1]=new header value
headersAll[content-length]=["0"]
headersAll[header1]=["new header value"]
headersSame=false
contextSame=true

feature6_request_routing.json.description: Construct an HTTP request from a method and an absolute requested URI, and observe the routing fields. The url is the resource path relative to the current handler (plus the original query); handlerPath is the root-relative path to the current handler. They can be inferred from one another, with a trailing slash added to a non-empty handler path. Changing the path moves a leading segment from url into handlerPath. Invalid combinations and malformed arguments are reported as neutral errors. The protocol version defaults to 1.1.

cases.expected_output:
error=invalid_argument
field=requestedUri
message=must be an absolute URL.

feature7_responses.json.description: Construct responses using their semantic factories and observe the resulting status code, body and key headers. Success and arbitrary-status responses carry the supplied body. Redirect responses set a location header and the corresponding 3xx status. Error responses default their body and set a text/plain content-type when no body is supplied, preserving any existing content-type parameters. A not-modified response uses status 304, carries a date header and an empty body. A status code below 100 is rejected as a neutral error.

cases.expected_output:
status=500
contentType=text/plain
location=null
hasDate=false
body=Internal Server Error

feature8_date_headers.json.description: Parse conditional date headers into timestamps. A request exposes its if-modified-since header as a parsed UTC timestamp; a response exposes its expires and last-modified headers likewise. When the corresponding header is absent the value is null.

feature9_cascade.json.description: Compose several handlers into a cascade that returns the first acceptable response. By default a response is unacceptable (causing the cascade to try the next handler) when its status is 404 or 405; otherwise it is returned immediately. If all handlers are unacceptable the final response is returned. The set of cascading statuses can be replaced by an explicit list or by a predicate over the status code. An empty cascade and the simultaneous use of both a status list and a predicate are reported as neutral errors.

_constraints.output_rule: Emit single-valued view (key=Value) and multi-valued view (keyAll=[...]) in sorted order.
Single view joins with commas; Multi view stores as singleton array.

_constraints.input_condition: Header value is an array of strings (e.g., ['B1','B2'])
_constraints.output_rule: Single view joins with commas; Multi view stores as array.

_constraints.input_condition: Body is array of ints or string
_constraints.output_rule: Content length is byte length of encoded body, overriding any header.
Content length is null.
Content length is null unless explicitly set in headers.

_constraints.input_condition: No content-type header present or body provided
_constraints.output_rule: Media type defaults to application/octet-stream.
MIME type extracts bare type (parameter stripped). Encoding includes charset value. Encoding parameter is added even if originally absent if explicit encoding provided.
Returns raw byte values (array of ints) unchanged.
Decodes bytes to string using precedence: explicit encoding -> content-type charset -> UTF-8.

_constraints.input_condition: Body is bytes/string to be read
_constraints.output_rule: Body can be read only once. Reads > 1st emit error.
exact_values: The 'read' method can only be called once on a shelf.Request/shelf.Response object.

_constraints.output_rule: Protocol defaults to 1.1. URL must be absolute. If relative, return invalid_argument.
Returns neutral error on field 'requestedUri'.
handlerPath gets trailing slash appended if non-empty.
Status 500. Body 'Internal Server Error'. Content-Type: text/plain.
Status 302. Location set to provided path. Empty body.
Status 304. Empty body. Date header present.
Rejected with neutral error.
Parses 'if-modified-since' header to UTC timestamp.
Parses 'expires' and 'last-modified'. Missing headers return null.
Response is unacceptable; continue to next handler in cascade.
Response is acceptable; cascade stops immediately.
Returns neutral error.

_constraints.input_condition: Cascade specifies both statusCodes list AND statusPredicate
_constraints.output_rule: Returns neutral error.
Request phases: Outermost-first (A, then B). Response phases: Innermost-first (B, then A). Handler runs after request phase.
Inner handler and response phase are skipped.
Error passed to middleware error phase, which may catch or rethrow.
Adds 'transfer-encoding: chunked' header.

_constraints.input_condition: Response has content-length OR status is 204/304 OR status < 200 OR status > 399 OR transfer-encoding present and non-identity
_constraints.output_rule: Passes through unchanged.
Returns neutral error.
headersSame=false, contextSame=true (shares underlying instances).

_constraints.input_condition: Change sets header/context to 'null' or empty list
_constraints.output_rule: Entry is removed from the new message.
Keys in output must be in ascending alphabetical order.

_constraints.input_condition: No explicit encoding and no charset in content-type
_constraints.output_rule: Decoding attempts UTF-8.
Might contain generic handler types like 'trace', 'fail', 'ok'.

_constraints.input_condition: Response phase of middleware calls respond()
_constraints.output_rule: Blocks outer middleware response phase.
